question: Compare the NuScale operating parameters in Table 1.2-1 with the detailed RCS temperatures in Table 5.1-2.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section outlines the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It explains the types of interface requirements (conceptual design information and COL information items), and specifies that COL applicants must address site-specific information and any departures from the certified design. Tables referenced in the section summarize these interfaces and list the required COL information items.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It accounts for uncertainties in flow resistance and core bypass, ensuring efficient operation, maximized electrical generation, and safety margins, with the system designed for low pressure losses to support natural circulation.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides an overview of the NuScale plant design, summarizing key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. It explains that each COL applicant must reference the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific details, with certain conceptual design information and security-related content clearly identified and delineated.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It explains that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, and outlines the procedures for valve testing, position verification, and a one-time in-situ test of DHRS heat removal capability during initial startup.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operation and Maintenance (OM) Code cases, as mandated by 10 CFR 50.55a and related regulatory guides. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC conditions, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, with instrumentation providing real-time pressure and level data to control systems.

nuscale_ch01_0679.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.1 AssumptionsInitial Power Level and Plant Operating Mode
nuscale_ch01_0679.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for steam system piping failures inside and outside containment, as outlined in DSRS 15.1.5. It confirms that the plant meets all specific criteria and assumptions—including initial power level, plant operating mode, loss of offsite power, postulated steam line break effects, and worst-case single active component failure—with no exceptions noted. The evaluation also highlights that critical heat flux is used for core damage assessment, and all safety-related classifications and radiological criteria are met.

nuscale_ch01_0676.title: DSRS 15.1.5, Rev 0: Steam System Piping Failures Inside and Outside of ContainmentII.2 Specific CriteriaEvaluation of Core Damage Potential
nuscale_ch01_0676.summary: This section confirms that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report meets the regulatory criteria for evaluating core damage potential due to steam system piping failures both inside and outside containment. It specifically notes that NuScale uses critical heat flux as a more accurate indicator of plant behavior than departure from nucleate boiling, and that all relevant assumptions and criteria are in conformance with regulatory standards.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core by natural circulation and interfaces with several other systems for safety and operational functions, including degasification and emergency core cooling. Its pressure-retaining parts help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, preventing the release of radionuclides.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0016.title: Principal Site Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0016.summary: This section provides a general overview of the NuScale plant design, outlining key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, and waste management. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and supplement it with site-specific information, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI) that are outside the certified scope. Security-related details are specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

edges.description: Section A references Table 1.2-1, which lists NuScale operating parameters, while Section B discusses the establishment of steady-state RCS temperatures (detailed in Table 5.1-2), thus the operating parameters in A provide the context or input values for the detailed temperature evaluations in B.
